questions.stem: On the MMPI-2, the K scale is sometimes added to certain clinical scales (such as scales 1, 4, 7, 8, and 9) to adjust for the test-taker's level of defensiveness. In statistical terms, when K corrections are applied in this manner, the K scale is functioning as a:
questions.explanation: When the K scale correction is added to certain clinical scales, it modifies the relationship between the raw clinical scale score and the interpretation of psychopathology. A moderator variable changes the strength or direction of the relationship between a predictor and an outcome. By adjusting clinical scale scores based on the level of defensiveness, K moderates how clinical scale scores are interpreted—a given raw score on Scale 8 means something different depending on the person's K score. A mediator would explain the mechanism through which one variable affects another. A confounding variable is an uncontrolled third variable that creates a spurious relationship. A dependent variable is the outcome being measured.
questions.kn_explanation: The MMPI-2 K-correction is a specific feature of a commonly used psychological instrument, fitting KN32's focus on commonly used instruments and their appropriate use.
questions.quality_comment: Conceptually rigorous question, but the lesson content does not explicitly describe K as a moderator variable or discuss K-corrections being added to clinical scales. The lesson only describes K as measuring 'subtle underreporting' and 'defensiveness.'
questions.suggestedParagraph: ## Validity Scales: Your Built-In Lie Detector
questions.lessonExcerpt: K (Defensiveness) | Subtle underreporting | More refined attempt to look good; may resist evaluation
